pages.title: Generalities on functors of modules
pages.html:
1 Generalities on functors of modules

-3mm

Let\(A\) be a commutative noetherian ring,
\(\underline{\mathrm{C}}\) the category of \(A\)-modules of finite type,
\(\underline{\mathrm{C}}'\) the category of arbitrary \(A\)-modules,
\(\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) the category of abelian groups.

The aim of this section is the study of certain properties of the functors \(T: \underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) (assumed additive).

Let us note that if \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}\), \(T(M)\) can be endowed in a canonical way with a structure of \(A\)-module which is the following: if \(f_{M}\) is the homothety of \(M\) associated with \(f\in A\), \(A\) acts on \(T(M)\) by \(f_{T(M)}\). In other words, \(T\) factors as:

\[ \xymatrix{ {\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}}\ar^{T}[rr]\ar_{T_{\circ}}[dr]&&\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\\ &{\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\prime}}\ar[ur]&} \]

where \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) is the canonical functor.

In what follows, \(T(M)\) will be considered as endowed with its structure of \(A\)-module.

By composing the morphism \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(A, M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(T(M), T(A))\) with the isomorphism \(M\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(A, M)\), one obtains the following morphisms, which are deduced from one another in an obvious manner:

\begin{align*} M&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(T(M), T(A)),\\ M\times T(M)&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(A),\\ T(M)&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, T(A)), \end{align*}

which defines for us a morphism \(\varphi_{T}\) of contravariant functors:

\begin{eqnarray*} \varphi_{T}: T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, T(A)). \end{eqnarray*}
Proposition 1.1

The two following properties are equivalent:

  1. \(\varphi_{T}\) is an isomorphism of functors.

  2. \(T\) is left exact.

The implication (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii) is trivial.

The implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) results from the fact that for a morphism \(u:F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F'\) of two left exact functors \(F\) and \(F'\) from \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\), if \(u(A)\) is an isomorphism, then \(u\) is an isomorphism (one uses the fact that \(A\) is noetherian and hence that every \(A\)-module of finite type is of finite presentation).

Remark 1.2

This shows in particular that the functors \(T:{\underline{\mathrm{C}}'}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) which are representable are the functors which commute with arbitrary projective limits (over a preordered set, not necessarily filtered).

If \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}, \underline{\mathrm{Ab}})_{g}\) denotes the full subcategory of \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}, \underline{\mathrm{Ab}})\) whose objects are the left exact functors, one has proved the equivalence of the categories

\[ \underline{\mathrm{C}}' \overset{\thickapprox}{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}, \underline{\mathrm{Ab}})_{g} \]

by the functors quasi-inverse to one another

\[ H \rightsquigarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H) \]

and

\[ T(A) \leftarrowtail T. \]

Let now \(J\) be an ideal of \(A\), \(Y=V(J)\subset \operatorname{Spec}\, A\), and let us denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\) the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}\) whose objects are the \(A\)-modules of finite type \(M\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp} M\subset Y\). One has:

\[ \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}=\bigcup_{n}\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{(n)}, \]

where \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{(n)}\) is the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\) of the modules \(M\) such that \(J^{n}M=0\).

Proposition 1.3

With the same notations as before, let \(T:\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) be a functor. To \(H=\) is associated a natural morphism

\[ \varphi_{T}: T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H), \]

and the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\varphi_{T}\) is an isomorphism.

  2. The functor \(T\) is left exact.

Proof▾

a) Definition of \(\varphi_{T}\).

Let \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\). There exists an integer \(n\) such that \(J^{n}M=0\). Then \(M\) is an \(A/J^{n}\)-module, and if \(T_{n}\) denotes the restriction of \(T\) to \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{(n)}\), one knows how to define the morphism

\begin{eqnarray*} T_{n}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H_{n}), \ \hbox{where}\ H_{n}=T(A/J^{n})\;; \end{eqnarray*}
\[ {T(M)=T_{n}(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, \varinjlim H_{n})= \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H)}\tag*{whence} \]
\[ \varphi_{T}: T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H).\tag*{and} \]

b) Equivalence of (i) and (ii).

It is clear that (i) entails (ii). Suppose (ii) verified and let \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}^{(n)}\). One has seen that \(T_{n}(M)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H_{n})\), hence for every integer \(n'>n\) one has

\[ T(M)=T_{n}(M)=T_{n'}(M)=\varinjlim T_{n}(M) \]
\[ T(M)=\varinjlim \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H_{n}).\tag*{and} \]

Since it is here a matter of filtered inductive limits, one also has the isomorphism

\[ \varinjlim \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H_{n})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, \varinjlim H_{n}) = \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H). \]

If \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}'_{Y}\) denotes the category of \(A\)-modules, with support contained in \(Y\), but not necessarily of finite type, one still has the natural equivalence of categories: \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}'_{Y}\overset{\thickapprox}{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}} \operatorname{Hom} (\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}^{\circ}, \underline{\mathrm{Ab}})_{g}\).

Q.E.D.

Application

The notations being the same as before, let

\[ T^{*}:\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}} \]

be an exact \(\partial\)-functor. For every \(i\in \mathbf{Z}\), one sets \(H^{i}_{n}=T^{i}(A/J^{n})\) and \(H^{i}=\varinjlim H^{i}_{n}\).

Theorem 1.4

Let \(n\in \mathbf{Z}\). If there exists \(i_{0}\in \mathbf{Z}\) such that \(T^{i}=0\) for all \(i<i_{0}\), then the three following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(T^{i}=0\) for all \(i<n\).

  2. \(H^{i}=0\) for all \(i<n\).

  3. There exists a module \(M_{0}\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp} M_{0}=Y\) and \(T^{i}(M_{0})=0\) for all \(i<n\).

Proof▾

It is evident that (i) entails (ii) and (iii) (one takes \(M_{0}=A/J\)). Let us show by induction on \(n\) that (ii) entails (i); it is true for \(n=i_{0}\), and we suppose it proved up to rank \(n\). Suppose then that \(H^{i}=0\) for all \(i<n+1\); by the induction hypothesis one has \(T^{i}=0\) for \(i<n\), but \(T^{n-1}=0\) entails that \(T^{n}\) is a left exact functor and

\[ T^{n}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H^{n})=0. \]

Let us show then that (iii) entails (ii). It is again true for \(n=i_{0}\); suppose it proved up to rank \(n\): let \(M_{0}\) be an \(A\)-module of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp} M_{0}=Y\) and \(T^{i}(M_{0})=0\) for all \(i<n+1\); by the induction hypothesis one then has \(H^{i}=0\) for all \(i<n\); it remains to show that \(H^{n}=0\). But "\(H^{i}=0\) for all \(i<n\)" implies that \(T^{n-1}=0\) and hence that \(T^{n}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H^{n})\). One then has

\[ \operatorname{Ass} H^{n}=\operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}(M_{0}, H^{n})=\operatorname{Supp} M_{0}\cap \operatorname{Ass} H^{n}= \operatorname{Ass} H^{n} \]

since

\[ \operatorname{Ass} H^{n}\subset \operatorname{Supp} H^{n}\subset Y= \operatorname{Supp} M_{0}. \]

Whence \(T^{n}(M_{0})=0\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow} \operatorname{Ass} H^{n}=\emptyset \mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow} H^{n}=0\); which completes the proof.

Q.E.D.

pages.title: Characterization of exact functors
pages.html:
2 Characterization of exact functors

The ring \(A\) is always assumed to be noetherian and commutative. The notations are those of Proposition 2:

\[ Y=V(J), \quad T:\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}, \quad H=\varinjlim T(A/J^{n}), \]

where \(T\) is assumed to be a left exact functor, whence:

\[ T(M)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H). \]
Proposition 2.1

The following properties are equivalent:

  1. The functor \(T\) is exact,

  2. \(H\) is injective in \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}'\).

Proof▾

It evidently suffices to show that (i) implies (ii), that is, to prove that if the restriction to \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\) of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H)\) is an exact functor, then \(H\) is injective. But since \(A\) is noetherian, in order to show that \(H\) is injective, we may restrict ourselves to proving that every homomorphism \(f:N\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\) whose source is a finitely generated \(A\)-module \(N\), a submodule of a finitely generated \(A\)-module \(M\), extends to a homomorphism \(\overline{f}:M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\).

The definition of \(H\) and the fact that \(N\) is finitely generated imply that there exists an integer \(n\) such that \(J^{n}.f(N)=0\). Let us then endow \(M\) and \(N\) with the \(J\)-adic topology. The \(J\)-adic topology of \(N\) is equivalent to the topology induced by the \(J\)-adic topology of \(M\) (Krull's theorem). There thus exists \(V=J^{k}\cdot M\) such that

\[ U=V\cap N\subset J^{n}N. \]

We then have the factorization

\[ \xymatrix{ N\ar_{f}[d]\ar[r]&N/U\ar^{u}[dl]\\ H&}, \]

\(N/U\) and \(M/V\) are in \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\). The hypothesis thus allows us to extend \(u\) to \(\overline{u}\)

\[ \xymatrix{ N/U\ar_{u}[d]\ar@{^{(}->}[r]&M/V\ar^{\overline{u}}[dl]\\ H&}, \]

and \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M/V\xrightarrow{\overline{u}} H\) gives the desired extension \(\overline{f}\).

Q.E.D.
Corollary 2.2

Let \(K\) be an injective \(A\)-module; then the submodule \(H^{0}_{J}(K)\) of \(K\) consisting of the elements annihilated by a suitable power of \(J\) is injective.

Proof▾

It suffices to verify that the restriction to \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\) of the functor \( \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H^{0}_{J}(K))\) is an exact functor. Now let \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\); there exists \(k\) such that \(J^{k}\cdot M=0\), and the inclusion

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H^{0}_{J}(K))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, K) \]

is then an isomorphism. Whence the result, since \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, K)\) is exact.

Q.E.D.

pages.title: Study of the case where \(T\) is left exact and \(T(M)\) of finite type for every \(M\)
pages.html:
3 Study of the case where \(T\) is left exact and \(T(M)\) of finite type for every \(M\)

Let, as above

\[ T:\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}, \]

we now assume that \(T\) is left exact and that we have the factorization

\[ \xymatrix@C=6mm@R=6mm{ {\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}_{Y}}\ar[dr]\ar^{T}[rr]&&{\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\,.}\\ &{\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}}\ar[ur]&} \]

We thus know how to define \(T(T(M))=T\circ T(M)\), and the canonical morphism

\[ M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H), H) \]

defines a morphism

\[ M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T(M). \]
Proposition 3.1

The ring \(A\) being always assumed noetherian, if we make the additional hypothesis that \(A/J\) is artinian, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(T\) is left exact and, for every \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\), \(T(M)\) is of finite type and \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T(M)\) is an isomorphism.

  2. \(T\) is exact and, for every residue field \(k\) associated with a maximal ideal containing \(J\), we have \(T(k)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k\).

  3. We have \(T\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H)\) with \(H\) injective and, for every \(k\) as in (ii), we have \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, H)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k\).

  4. \(T\) is exact and, for every \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\), we have \(\operatorname{long} T(M)=\operatorname{long} M\).

Proof▾

We have already shown the equivalence of (ii) and (iii) (prop. 2.1). Let us show that (ii) implies (iv): first, if \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\), since \(M\) is an \(A/J^{n}\)-module with \(A/J^{n}\) artinian, \(\operatorname{long} M\) is finite. Let us argue by induction on the length of \(M\). Condition (iv) is true if \(\operatorname{long} M=1\), because then \(M\) is a \(k\). If \(\operatorname{long} M>1\), there exists a submodule \(M'\) of \(M\) such that \(M'\neq 0\) and \(\operatorname{long} M'<\operatorname{long} M\). Let us then form the exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]

Since \(T\) is exact, we have the sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M'')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

and \(\operatorname{long} T(M)=\operatorname{long} T(M')+\operatorname{long} T(M'')=\operatorname{long} M'+\operatorname{long} M''=\operatorname{long} M\).

(ii) implies (i): Since (ii) implies (iv), let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\); we have \(\operatorname{long} T(M)=\operatorname{long} M\); hence \(T(M)\) is of finite length and consequently of finite type.

It remains to show that \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T(M)\) is an isomorphism; we argue again by induction on \(\operatorname{long} M\). For \(M=k\) it is true. In the general case we write the commutative diagram whose two rows are exact:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&M'\ar[r]\ar[d]&M\ar[r]\ar[d]&M''\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]& T\circ T(M')\ar[r]& T\circ T(M)\ar[r]& T\circ T(M'')\ar[r]&0, } \]

where \(M'\) is a submodule of \(M\) such that \(M'\neq 0\) and \(\operatorname{long} M'<\operatorname{long} M\). By the induction hypothesis, the outer arrows are isomorphisms, hence

\[ M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T(M) \]

is an isomorphism.

(i) implies (ii): let

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

be an exact sequence of \(A\)-modules of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\), and let \(Q\) be the cokernel of \(T(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M')\). Let us apply \(T\) to the exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M'')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

we obtain:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&T(Q)\ar[r]&T\circ T(M')\ar[r]&T\circ T(M)&\\ & &M'\ar_{s}[u]\ar[r]&M\ar_{s}[u], } \]

hence \(T(Q)=0\) and \(Q\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} T(T(Q))=0\).

On the other hand, let \(k\) be a residue field, \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\), \(J\subset \mathfrak{m}\). We must show that \(T(k)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k\). For this it suffices to remark that \(T(k)\) is a \(k\)-vector space. We deduce from this:

\begin{gather*} T(k)\simeq k\oplus V,\\ T(T(k))\simeq T(k)\oplus T(V)\simeq k\oplus V\oplus T(V)\simeq k, \end{gather*}

whence \(V=0\).

Let us finally show that (iv) implies (iii): it suffices to show that \(T(k)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k\); now \(\operatorname{long} T(k)= \operatorname{long} k=1\), hence \(T(k)=k'\) is a residue field and \(\operatorname{Supp} k'= \operatorname{Supp} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, H)\subset \operatorname{Supp} k\). Hence \(k=k'\).

Q.E.D.
Remark 3.2

One can show that condition (iv) is equivalent to the condition

(iv)\('\) For every \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\), we have \(\operatorname{long} T(M)=\operatorname{long} M\).

blocks.html: 3 Study of the case where \(T\) is left exact and \(T(M)\) of finite type for every \(M\)
Let, as above
\[ T:\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}, \]
we now assume that \(T\) is left exact and that we have the factorization
\[ \xymatrix@C=6mm@R=6mm{ {\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}_{Y}}\ar[dr]\ar^{T}[rr]&&{\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\,.}\\ &{\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}}\ar[ur]&} \]
We thus know how to define \(T(T(M))=T\circ T(M)\), and the canonical morphism
\[ M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, H), H) \]
defines a morphism
\[ M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T(M). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1

The ring \(A\) being always assumed noetherian, if we make the additional hypothesis that \(A/J\) is artinian, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(T\) is left exact and, for every \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\), \(T(M)\) is of finite type and \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T(M)\) is an isomorphism.

  2. \(T\) is exact and, for every residue field \(k\) associated with a maximal ideal containing \(J\), we have \(T(k)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k\).

  3. We have \(T\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, H)\) with \(H\) injective and, for every \(k\) as in (ii), we have \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, H)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k\).

  4. \(T\) is exact and, for every \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\), we have \(\operatorname{long} T(M)=\operatorname{long} M\).

pages.title: Dualizing module. Dualizing functor
pages.html:
4 Dualizing Module. Dualizing Functor

Definition 4.1

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring and \(\mathfrak{m}\) its maximal ideal. A dualizing functor for \(A\) is any functor

\[ T:\underline{\mathrm{C}}^{\circ}_\mathfrak{m}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}, \]

where we write \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}}\) instead of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{Y}\) for \(Y=V(\mathfrak{m})\), which satisfies the equivalent conditions of proposition 3.1. We say that an \(A\)-module \(I\) is dualizing for \(A\) if the functor \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, I)\) is dualizing.

One can generalize definition 4.1 to the case where \(A\) is no longer assumed to be a local ring.

Definition 4.2

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring and let \(\overline{\underline{\mathrm{C}}}\) be the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}\) consisting of the \(A\)-modules of finite length; a dualizing functor is any \(A\)-linear functor \(T\) from \(\overline{\underline{\mathrm{C}}}^{\circ}\) to \(\overline{\underline{\mathrm{C}}}\) which is exact and such that the morphism of functors

\[ \operatorname{id} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T \]

is an isomorphism.

We are going to prove an existence theorem and also that the module \(I\) which represents such a functor is locally artinian. We shall also show that, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\), the \(\mathfrak{m}\)-primary component of the socle of \(I\) is of length 1.

Proposition 4.3

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be two noetherian local rings with maximal ideals \(\mathfrak{m}_{A}\) and \(\mathfrak{m}_{B}\), such that \(B\) is a finite \(A\)-algebra. Then, if \(I\) is a dualizing module for \(A\), \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(B, I)\) is a dualizing module for \(B\).

Proof▾

Let

\[ R: \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}_{B}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}_{A}} \]

be the restriction of scalars functor; it is exact. Let \(T\) be a dualizing functor for \(A\),

\[ T: \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}_{A}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}; \]

it is exact and, for every \(M\in \operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}_{A}}\), the natural morphism \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\circ T(M)\) is an isomorphism; hence \(T\circ R\) is a dualizing functor for \(B\). If \(I\) represents \(T\), then by the classical formula \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, I)=\operatorname{Hom}_{B}(M, \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(B, I))\), valid for every \(B\)-module \(M\), we deduce that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(B, I)\) is a dualizing module for \(B\).

Q.E.D.
Corollary 4.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring and \(\mathfrak{a}\) an ideal of \(A\); let \(B=A/\mathfrak{a}\). If \(I\) is a dualizing module for \(A\), the annihilator of \(\mathfrak{a}\) in \(I\) is a dualizing module for \(B\).

Lemma 4.5

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring and \(I\) a locally artinian \(A\)-module. There exists a canonical isomorphism

\[ I\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{I}=I\otimes_{A}\widehat{A}. \]
Proof▾

Let \(I_{n}\) be the annihilator of \(\mathfrak{m}^n\) in \(I\), where \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the maximal ideal of \(A\). To say that \(I\) is locally artinian is to say that \(I\) is the inductive limit of the \(I_{n}\) and that these are of finite length. Now the tensor product commutes with inductive limits, so we are reduced to the case where \(I\) is artinian. In this case \(I\) is annihilated by a power of the maximal ideal, say \(\mathfrak{m}^{k}\); hence for \(p\geqslant k\), \(I\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} I\otimes_{A}A/\mathfrak{m}^{p}\), hence \(I\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} I\otimes_{A}\widehat{A}\) since \(A\) is noetherian and \(I\) is of finite type.

We conclude that the functor of restriction of scalars from \(\widehat{A}\) to \(A\) and the functor of extension of scalars induce equivalences quasi-inverse to one another between the category of locally artinian \(\widehat{A}\)-modules and the category of locally artinian \(A\)-modules.

Q.E.D.
Proposition 4.6

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(\widehat{A}\) its completion, \(I\) a dualizing module for \(A\) (resp. for \(\widehat{A}\)) and \(J\) the completion of \(I\) (resp. the \(A\)-module obtained by restriction of scalars). Then \(J\) is a dualizing module for \(\widehat{A}\) (resp. for \(A\)). Moreover the abelian groups underlying \(I\) and \(J\) are isomorphic.

Proof▾

One simply remarks that the equivalence between the category of locally artinian \(A\)-modules and the category of locally artinian \(\widehat{A}\)-modules induces an isomorphism between the bifunctors \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, \;)\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{A}}(\;, \;)\), and that the characterization of a dualizing functor or module involves only these bifunctors.

Q.E.D.
Theorem 4.7

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring.

  1. There always exists a dualizing module \(I\).

  2. Two dualizing modules are isomorphic (by a non-canonical isomorphism).

  3. In order for a module \(I\) to be dualizing, it is necessary and sufficient that it be an injective envelope of the residue field \(k\) of \(A\).

Remark 4.8

Proposition 4.6 allows us to reduce to the case of a complete noetherian local ring. By a structure theorem of COHEN, such a ring is a quotient of a regular ring. Proposition 4.3 then allows us to assume \(A\) regular. As we shall see further on, this remark permits an explicit computation of the dualizing module1; we shall nevertheless prove theorem 4.7 by other means.

Recollections

Before proving the theorem, we make a few recollections on the notion of injective envelope. Cf. GABRIEL, Thèse, Paris 1961, Des Catégories Abéliennes, ch. II §5.

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be an abelian category in which the \(\varinjlim\) exist and are exact (e.g. \(\mathscr{C}=\)category of modules). Every object \(M\) embeds into an injective object, and an injective envelope of \(M\) is any minimal injective object containing \(M\). We have the following properties:

  1. Every object \(M\) has an injective envelope \(I\).

  2. If \(I\) and \(J\) are two injective envelopes of \(M\), there exists between \(I\) and \(J\) an isomorphism (in general not unique) which induces the identity on \(M\).

  3. \(I\) is an essential extension of \(M\), that is, \(P\subset I\) and \(P\cap M=\{ 0\}\) imply (\(P=\{ 0\}\)). Moreover if \(I\) is injective and an essential extension of \(M\), \(I\) is an injective envelope of \(M\).

Granting these results, to prove theorem 4.7 it evidently suffices to prove c).

Proof▾

Let \(I\) be a dualizing module for \(A\). Then \(I\) is injective and \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\) is isomorphic to \(k\). Composing the isomorphism \(k\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\) with the inclusion

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(A, I)\simeq I, \]

we obtain the inclusion

\[ k\hookrightarrow I. \]

Let us show that \(I\) is an injective envelope of \(k\). Let \(J\) be an injective module such that

\[ k\subset J\subset I. \]

Since \(J\) is injective, there exists an injective \(A\)-submodule \(J'\) of \(I\) such that \(I=J\oplus J'\). Let us show that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J')=0\). We have

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J)\oplus \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J'); \]

\(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J)\) is a vector subspace of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\simeq k\) not reduced to zero (since it contains the inclusion \(k\subset J\)), hence \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J)\simeq k\) and consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J')=0\).

Arguing by induction on the length we deduce that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, J')=0\) for every \(A\)-module \(M\) of finite length; hence \(J'\) is the object which represents the functor \(0:\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\), and consequently \(J'=0\).

Conversely, let \(I\) be an injective envelope of \(k\). To see that \(I\) is a dualizing module, it suffices to show that \(V=\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\) is isomorphic to \(k\). Now we have the double inclusion

\[ k\subset V\subset I; \]

\(V\) is a vector space over \(k\) which decomposes as the direct sum of \(k\) and a vector subspace \(V'\) of \(I\) such that \(V'\cap k=0\). Now \(I\) is an essential extension of \(k\), whence \(V'=0\) and \(V=k\).

Q.E.D.
Corollary 4.9

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; every dualizing module for \(A\) is locally artinian.

Proof▾

Let \(I\) be a dualizing module; it is an injective envelope of \(k\). Let us use the notation and the result of corollary 2.2. We have

\[ k\subset H^{0}_{\mathfrak{m}}(I)\subset I, \]

and \(H^{0}_{\mathfrak{m}}(I)\) is injective. We deduce that \(I=H^{0}_{\mathfrak{m}}(I)\) and hence that \(I\) is locally artinian.

Q.E.D.

blocks.html:
Proof▾

Let \(I\) be a dualizing module for \(A\). Then \(I\) is injective and \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\) is isomorphic to \(k\). Composing the isomorphism \(k\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\) with the inclusion

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(A, I)\simeq I, \]

we obtain the inclusion

\[ k\hookrightarrow I. \]

Let us show that \(I\) is an injective envelope of \(k\). Let \(J\) be an injective module such that

\[ k\subset J\subset I. \]

Since \(J\) is injective, there exists an injective \(A\)-submodule \(J'\) of \(I\) such that \(I=J\oplus J'\). Let us show that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J')=0\). We have

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J)\oplus \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J'); \]

\(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J)\) is a vector subspace of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\simeq k\) not reduced to zero (since it contains the inclusion \(k\subset J\)), hence \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J)\simeq k\) and consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, J')=0\).

Arguing by induction on the length we deduce that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, J')=0\) for every \(A\)-module \(M\) of finite length; hence \(J'\) is the object which represents the functor \(0:\underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}}^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\), and consequently \(J'=0\).

Conversely, let \(I\) be an injective envelope of \(k\). To see that \(I\) is a dualizing module, it suffices to show that \(V=\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(k, I)\) is isomorphic to \(k\). Now we have the double inclusion

\[ k\subset V\subset I; \]

\(V\) is a vector space over \(k\) which decomposes as the direct sum of \(k\) and a vector subspace \(V'\) of \(I\) such that \(V'\cap k=0\). Now \(I\) is an essential extension of \(k\), whence \(V'=0\) and \(V=k\).

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; every dualizing module for \(A\) is locally artinian.

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Let \(I\) be a dualizing module; it is an injective envelope of \(k\). Let us use the notation and the result of corollary 2.2. We have

\[ k\subset H^{0}_{\mathfrak{m}}(I)\subset I, \]

and \(H^{0}_{\mathfrak{m}}(I)\) is injective. We deduce that \(I=H^{0}_{\mathfrak{m}}(I)\) and hence that \(I\) is locally artinian.

Q.E.D.

footnotes.html: This was the method followed by Grothendieck (in 1957). The method by injective envelopes that follows is due, it seems, to K.  Morita, Sc. Rep. Tokyo Kyoiku Daigaku, t.  6, 1958-59, p.  83-142. Morita's work is moreover independent of Grothendieck's and well prior to the present seminar, and is not limited to the case of commutative base rings.

pages.title: Consequences of the theory of dualizing modules
pages.html:
5 Consequences of the theory of dualizing modules

The functor

\[ T=\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, I)\colon \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathfrak{m}} \]

is an anti-equivalence. Indeed \(T\circ T\) is isomorphic to the identity functor and the argument is formal from there.

From this one deduces the usual properties of the notion of orthogonality:

Let \(M^{*}=\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(M, I)=T(M)\) and let \(N\subset M\) be a submodule. One calls orthogonal of \(N\) the submodule \(N'\) of \(M^{*}\) consisting of the elements of \(M^*\) vanishing on \(N\). One thus obtains a bijection between the set of submodules of \(M\) and the set of submodules of \(M^{*}\), which reverses the ordering.

In particular one has:

  • \(\operatorname{long}_{M}N=\operatorname{colong}_{M^{*}}N'\).

  • To the monogenic modules, i.e. those such that \(M/\mathfrak{m} M\) is of dimension 0 or 1, there correspond in the duality the modules whose socle is of length 0 or 1.

  • If \(A\) is artinian, the ideals of \(A\) correspond to the submodules of \(I\).

    etc.

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(DA\) the category of \(A\)-modules \(M\) such that, for all \(n\in {\bf N}\), \(M^{(n)}=M/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}M\) is of finite length and such that \(M=\varprojlim_{n}M^{(n)}\), and let \(\widehat{A}\) be the completion of \(A\). The restriction-of-scalars functor and the completion functor are quasi-inverse equivalences between \(DA\) and \(D\widehat{A}\), which commute up to isomorphism with the formation of the abelian groups underlying the modules under consideration. Let us denote by \(CA\) the category of locally artinian \(A\)-modules with socle of finite dimension.

Proposition 5.1

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring and let \(I\) be a dualizing module for \(A\). The functors

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\;, I)\colon (CA)^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} DA \]

and

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{A}}(\;, I)\colon DA\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (CA)^{\circ} \]

are equivalences of categories, quasi-inverse to one another.

Moreover, if one transports these functors via the equivalences of categories between \(DA\) and \(D\widehat{A}\) on the one hand, and \(CA\) and \(C\widehat{A}\) on the other, one finds the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{A}}(\;, I)\).

Proof▾

Let \(X\in \operatorname{Ob} CA\). By definition, one has:

\[ X=\varinjlim_{k\in {\bf N}}X_{k}, \quad X_{k}=\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{k+1}, X), \]

hence

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(X, I)=\varprojlim \operatorname{Hom}_{A}(X_{k}, I). \]

Hence \(Y=\varprojlim X_{k}\) is a \(\widehat{A}\)-module of finite type as follows from \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{I}}\) 7.2.9. We remark in this connection that \(D\widehat{A}\) is also the category of \(\widehat{A}\)-modules of finite type or, if one prefers, that \(DA\) is the category of \(A\)-modules complete and of finite type over \(\widehat{A}\). Let then \(Y\) be such a module, let \(f:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I\) be a \(\widehat{A}\)-homomorphism. The image of \(f\) is a submodule of finite type, hence is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}^{k}\) for some \(k\); indeed every \(x\in I\) is annihilated by a power of \(\mathfrak{m}\). Hence \(f\) factors through \(y/\mathfrak{m}^{k}Y\), whence it follows that

\begin{align*} \operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{A}}(Y, I)&=\varinjlim_k\operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{A}}(Y^{(k)}, I)\quad\text{with } Y^{(k)}=Y/\mathfrak{m}^{k+1}Y\\ &=\varinjlim_k(Y^{(k)})^* \end{align*}

belongs to \(\operatorname{Ob} CA\). Whence it follows immediately that the two functors of the statement are quasi-inverse to one another.

Q.E.D.

It follows from the preceding considerations that one changes nothing in the categories or in the functors under consideration, nor in the abelian groups underlying the modules under consideration, by replacing \(A\) by \(\widehat{A}\); proposition 5.1 is then stated as follows:

The restriction of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{A}}(\;, I)\) to the category of \(\widehat{A}\)-modules of finite type takes its values in the category of locally artinian \(\widehat{A}\)-modules with socle of finite dimension, and admits a quasi-inverse functor, which is the restriction of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{A}}(\;, I)\). On the intersection of these two categories, these two functors coincide (obviously!) and establish a self-duality of the category of \(\widehat{A}\)-modules of finite length.

Example 5.2 (MACAULAY)

Let \(A\) be a local ring with residue field \(k\). Let \(k_{0}\) be a subfield of \(A\) such that \(k\) is finite over \(k_{0}\), \([k:k_{0}]=d\). Every \(A\)-module of finite length can be regarded as a \(k_{0}\)-vector space of finite dimension equal to \(d\cdot\operatorname{long}(M)\). The functor \(T\):

\[ M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{k_{0}}(M, k_{0}) \]

is then exact and preserves length, hence is dualizing for \(A\). The associated dualizing module is therefore:

\[ A'=\varinjlim_{n}\operatorname{Hom}_{k_{0}}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{n}, k_{0}), \]

it is the topological dual of \(A\) equipped with the \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic topology.

Example 5.3

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring regular of dimension \(n\). Let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be its maximal ideal, let \(k\) be its residue field. There exists a regular system of parameters, \((x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n})\), which generates \(\mathfrak{m}\), and which is an \(A\)-regular sequence. One can therefore compute the \(\operatorname{Ext}^{i}_{A}(k, A)\) by the KOSZUL complex; one finds:

\begin{align*} \operatorname{Ext}^{i}_{A}(k, A)&=0\quad \text{if } i\neq n,\\ \operatorname{Ext}^{n}_{A}(k, A)&\simeq k. \end{align*}

The depth of \(A\) being \(n\), for every \(M\) annihilated by a power of \(\mathfrak{m}\), \(\operatorname{Ext}^{i}_{A}(M, A)=0\) if \(i<n\); moreover \(\operatorname{Ext}^{i}_{A}(M, A)=0\) if \(i>n\) since the global cohomological dimension of \(A\) is equal to \(n\). Hence \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n}_{A}(\;, A)\) is exact and moreover \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n}_{A}(k, A)\simeq k\); it follows that:

Proposition 5.4

If \(A\) is a regular noetherian local ring of dimension \(n\), the functor

\[ M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{A}^{n}(M, A) \]

is dualizing. The associated dualizing module is

\[ I=\varinjlim_{r}\operatorname{Ext}_{A}^{n}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{r}, A), \]

it is isomorphic to \(H^{n}_{\mathfrak{m}}(A)\) (Exposé II, th. 6)1.

Remark 5.5

If \(A\) satisfies at the same time the hypotheses of the two preceding examples, the two dualizing modules found are isomorphic. Suppose for example that \(A\) is regular of dimension \(n\), complete and of equal characteristics. There then exists a field of representatives, say \(K\). If one chooses a system of parameters \((x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n})\) of \(A\), one can construct an isomorphism between \(A\) and the ring of formal power series: \(K[[T_{1}, \ldots, T_{n}]]\); whence, as we shall see, an explicit isomorphism between the two dualizing modules

\[ v\colon H^{n}(A)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'. \]

One can find an intrinsic interpretation of this isomorphism by means of the completed module \(\Omega^{n}(A/K)\) of relative differentials of maximal degree. Indeed, it is known that \(\Omega^{n}(A/K)\) admits a basis consisting of the element \(dx_{1}\wedge dx_{2}\cdots \wedge dx_{n}\).

Whence an isomorphism

\[ u\colon H^{n}(\Omega^{n}(A/K))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^{n}(A). \]

A remarkable fact is then that the composite

\[ vu=w\colon H^{n}(\Omega^{n})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A' \]

no longer depends on the choice of the system of parameters and commutes with the change of the base field.

To construct \(v\) one computes \(H^{n}(A)\) by means of the KOSZUL complex associated with the \(x_{i}\), one finds:

\[ H^{n}(A)=\varinjlim_{r}A/(x_{1}^{r}, \ldots, x_{n}^{r}); \]

where the transition morphisms are defined as follows: set \(I_{r}=A/(x_{1}^{r}, \ldots, x_{n}^{r})\); let \(e_{a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}}^{r}\) be the image of \((x_{1})^{a_{1}}(x_{2})^{a_{2}}\cdots (x_{n})^{a_{n}}\) in \(I_{r}\). The \(e_{a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}}^{r}\), for \(s\leqslant a_{i}<r\) form a basis of \(I_{r}\).

That said, if \(s\) is an integer, the transition morphism

\[ t_{r, r+s}\colon I_{r}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I_{r+s} \]

is multiplication by \(x_{1}^{s}x_{2}^{s}\cdots x_{n}^{s}\), hence:

\[ u_{r, r+s}(e_{a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}}^{r})=e_{a_{1}+s, \ldots, a_{n}+s}^{r+s}. \]

Note that the datum of a \(A\)-homomorphism \(w\) from a \(A\)-module \(M\) into \(A'\) is equivalent to the datum of a \(K\)-linear form \(w':M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) which is continuous on the submodules of finite type. In the case \(M=H^{n}(\Omega^{n})\), the definition of \(w\) is therefore equivalent to that of a linear form

\[ \rho \colon H^{n}(\Omega^{n})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K, \]

called the residue form2. To construct \(\rho\), it suffices to define forms \(\rho_{r}:I_{r}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) which glue together, and one will take

\[ \rho_{r}(e_{a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}}^{r})= \begin{cases} 1&\text{if}\ a_{i}=r-1\ \text{for}\ 1\leqslant i\leqslant n\\ 0& \text{otherwise.} \end{cases} \]
